summary: load_and_resolve processes all bare objects, then all archives, then all shared objects — violating command-line input order. Archives see undefined symbols from objects that appear after them on the command line, and can pull members to satisfy symbols that a subsequent shared library was supposed to satisfy, producing an incorrect DT_NEEDED list.
evidence: Lines 269-297: The function partitions inputs into three groups (bare, archives, shared objects) and processes each group entirely before moving to the next. With input order `foo.o libc.so libstatic.a`: (1) foo.o is processed, marking malloc as undefined; (2) libstatic.a is processed, pulling any member that defines malloc; (3) libc.so is processed, but register_shared_exports (line 294) only counts symbols that remain undefined—since malloc is already defined by the archive member, it returns 0, and libc.so is omitted from needed. The correct left-to-right semantics would have libc.so satisfy malloc as an import first, preventing the archive member from being pulled.

summary: `--defsym` values for linker-provided symbol names (e.g. `_end`, `_edata`, `_GLOBAL_OFFSET_TABLE_`) are silently overwritten by `set_linker_addresses` after layout.
evidence:
Call sequence confirmed in source:
1. `predefine_linker_symbols` (line 214): if `_end` is an undefined reference, calls `define_absolute("_end", 0)` and pushes `"_end"` into `provided`.
2. `apply_defsym` (line 215): calls `define_absolute("_end", 0x1234)` — `define_absolute` unconditionally overwrites (documented as "Define (or overwrite)"), so value becomes `0x1234`.
3. `set_linker_addresses` (line 244): iterates `provided`, finds `"_end"`, and stamps `r.value = layout.end` / `r.virtual_address = layout.end` — unconditionally overwriting `0x1234` with the layout-derived address.
Net result: `--defsym _end=0x1234` is completely ignored when `_end` appears as an undefined reference in any input object.

summary: parse_linker_script token filter admits directory-path arguments (e.g. SEARCH_DIR) via the t.contains('/') predicate, causing resolve_script_ref to resolve real directories onto the work queue; is_linker_script then fails to read the directory and returns false, so the directory is pushed into the final output list as if it were an object file.
evidence: Line 562: `t.starts_with("-l") || t.contains('/') || t.ends_with(".a") || t.contains(".so")`. A linker script clause `SEARCH_DIR(/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu)` has its parens replaced with spaces (line 558), yielding token `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu`. This satisfies `t.contains('/')` and enters the returned Vec. In `resolve_script_ref` (line 599), `p.is_absolute() && p.exists()` is true for any existing directory, so `Some(directory_path)` is returned and pushed to `work` (line 531). Back in `expand_inputs`, `is_linker_script` calls `std::fs::read` on the directory, which fails (Err on a directory fd), returns `false` (line 541-542), and the directory is pushed to `out` (line 525) as a final input file — producing a downstream I/O error when the caller tries to parse it as an object.

summary: strip_block_comments silently discards content after an unterminated /* comment
evidence: In strip_block_comments (lines 567-584), when a /* comment lacks a closing */, the inner while loop (line 574) exits when i+1 >= b.len() is reached, without finding */. Control then executes i += 2 (line 577), moving i beyond the buffer length. The outer loop (line 571) then exits without processing any remaining bytes. Tested with input "hello /* world": correctly outputs "hello " with the unterminated comment and any following content lost. This causes silent data loss when parsing malformed linker scripts or binary files mistaken for scripts.

summary: try_reuse propagates an I/O error (CacheError::Io) rather than returning Ok(false) when the manifest file disappears between the path.exists() check and the subsequent fs::read call.
evidence: Line 123 checks `if !path.exists() || !output.exists() { return Ok(false); }` to detect missing cache files. However, line 126–129 immediately calls `std::fs::read(&path).map_err(|e| CacheError::Io { ... })?`, which propagates an I/O error if the file is deleted or becomes inaccessible in the narrow window between the existence check and the read. This is a classic TOCTOU (Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use) race. By contrast, lines 147–150 and 154–157 demonstrate the established pattern: match on Fingerprint::of_file() results and return Ok(false) on any file-access error, treating transient or missing files as a cache miss rather than a fatal error. The manifest read should follow the same pattern.

summary: No test covers the corrupted/truncated-manifest recovery path in try_reuse
evidence: Confirmed in peony-cache/src/lib.rs:130-134, try_reuse decodes the manifest and on a bincode decode error returns Ok(false) ("unreadable/stale cache → relink"). The incremental cache IS exercised by integration tests — peony/tests/link_and_run.rs:114 `incremental_reuse_and_relink` (reuse + relink on input change) and peony/tests/more_cases.rs:193 `incremental_partial_change` (relink on changed input) — but none of them write a corrupted/truncated manifest.bin and assert the linker falls back to a correct relink. The in-crate unit tests (peony-cache/src/lib.rs:174-199) only cover Fingerprint determinism/length and cache_dir's `.incr` extension; they do not call try_reuse at all. So the Err(_) => Ok(false) branch is genuinely untested. Because this branch is correctness-critical (a regression to `?` or to returning Ok(true) on garbage could either panic or silently reuse an invalid cached output), the gap is more than cosmetic.

summary: No test exercises include_archive_members with a zero-member archive
evidence: include_archive_members (lines 364-384) correctly handles an empty archive: the population loop adds nothing to `members`, and the fixpoint loop either breaks immediately on `undefined.is_empty()` or on `!included_any`. The only archive integration test, `static_archive` in peony/tests/link_and_run.rs (lines 95-109), always supplies two members. A grep across all test files finds no test that passes an archive with zero members. The edge case is untested.

summary: Silent fallback for invalid --threads value without warning to user
evidence: Line 110: `a.threads = take(&argv, &mut i, arg)?.parse().unwrap_or(0)` and line 124: `a.threads = arg[10..].parse().unwrap_or(0)`. Both the space-separated (`--threads abc`) and equals-separated (`--threads=abc`) forms silently swallow a parse failure and default to 0 (= use all cores). No error, warning (`tracing::warn!`), or `eprintln!` is emitted. The doc comment on line 9 documents `0 = all cores`, so a user passing a typo like `--threads 4x` will silently get all cores instead of the intended 4 threads, with no indication anything went wrong.

summary: resolve_inputs collapses command-line ordering by appending ALL -l libraries after ALL positional inputs, breaking GNU ld's left-to-right link semantics at the argument-parsing layer.
evidence: In parse_args (lines 85-137), -l flags are routed to args.libraries (line 108) and positional files to args.inputs (line 132), separating them. Then in resolve_inputs (lines 613-631), line 614 clones args.inputs and lines 615-626 append each resolved library afterward in a single loop. For command `obj1.o -lfoo obj2.o`, parse_args creates inputs=[obj1.o, obj2.o] and libraries=[foo]. resolve_inputs then produces [obj1.o, obj2.o, libfoo.a/so], moving the library from its original position (between obj1.o and obj2.o) to the end. GNU ld would process -lfoo at its exact command-line position, allowing it to satisfy only obj1.o's undefined symbols, not obj2.o's. The difference is architecturally upstream from (and independent of) the three-phase partition in load_and_resolve, making it a distinct defect.

summary: --build-id=none (the standard ld form for DISABLING a build-id) is matched by the arg.starts_with("--build-id") prefix arm and unconditionally sets build_id = true, inverting user intent
evidence: Line 114 correctly handles bare `--build-id` flag. Line 126 uses a prefix match `arg.starts_with("--build-id")` that unconditionally sets `build_id = true` for ALL `--build-id*` variants, including `--build-id=none`. Per GNU ld semantics (confirmed in mold/docs/mold.md), `--build-id=none` should DISABLE the build-id, and `--no-build-id` is a synonym. The code has no special handling for the `=none` suffix and no `--no-build-id` handler. This causes explicit user requests to suppress build-ids (for reproducibility) to be silently inverted.

summary: expand_inputs lacks a visited set: a cyclic linker-script graph (A→B→A) causes an infinite loop, and a diamond or repeated reference causes duplicate entries in the output list.
evidence: Lines 519–537 (verified in source): `expand_inputs` drives a `VecDeque<PathBuf>` named `work`. At line 531, every resolved reference from `parse_linker_script` is pushed back to `work` with no check against a seen-set. If parse_linker_script(A) returns B and parse_linker_script(B) returns A (mutual GROUP inclusion), A is re-queued, parsed again, B is re-queued, and the `while let Some(p) = work.pop_front()` loop never terminates — a hard hang. Additionally, any non-script path reached via two routes (diamond topology or a script listing the same object twice) is pushed to `out` at line 525 without deduplication, producing duplicate entries downstream. Both issues are confirmed absent from the code: there is no `HashSet`, `BTreeSet`, or `.contains()` guard anywhere in the function.

summary: -Bstatic/-Bdynamic/--static flags are silently dropped, and resolve_inputs always prefers lib<name>.so over lib<name>.a with no link-mode awareness, so -Bstatic -lfoo silently resolves to the shared object when both exist.
evidence: IGNORE_WITH_VALUE (lines 87–91) does not include -Bstatic, -Bdynamic, or --static, and no dedicated match arm handles them. They fall through to `_ if arg.starts_with('-') => {}` (line 131) and are silently discarded. The Args struct (lines 46–60) has no link-mode field. resolve_inputs (line 620) always iterates `[lib{name}.so, lib{name}.a]` in that order via flat_map and returns the first existing file, with no awareness of any requested static-link mode. A caller passing `-Bstatic -lfoo` would get libfoo.so linked when both files are present, contradicting the explicit request.

summary: Work-stealing termination flag `done` is set true immediately after spawning workers, so idle workers abandon stealing the moment their own deque drains — defeating the advertised load-balanced dispatch (though no work is dropped).
evidence: In write_section_data_parallel, all work items are seeded round-robin into per-worker deques (line 297) BEFORE any thread starts. Inside std::thread::scope, the spawn loop calls the non-blocking scope.spawn (line 311), then `done.store(true, Ordering::Release)` runs at line 365 — i.e. essentially immediately, before workers have processed their items; the real join happens only when the scope closure returns. Each worker loop pops its own deque (line 316), and only consults `done` (line 329) after worker.pop() yields None AND every steal attempt failed. Crucially, lines 322-323 treat `Steal::Empty | Steal::Retry => {}` identically and do NOT retry. Per the ws-deque crate (src/lib.rs steal_from, lines 642-644), `Steal::Retry` is returned on a lost CAS race when items may still exist (the crate's own pop_fifo does `Steal::Retry => continue`). So a worker that drains its own deque, then sees a transient Empty/Retry from each sibling while `done` is already true, breaks out (line 329) instead of waiting for a busy sibling to expose stealable work. No items are dropped — each owner fully drains its own deque via worker.pop(), whose internal pop_fifo retries on contention — but the Chase-Lev work-stealing never rebalances a slow/large section onto an idle thread, so the 'load-balanced dispatch' promised in comments (lines 126, 254-255) does not occur.

summary: In gc_sections' S3-GC parallel BFS, `done.store(true)` runs immediately after spawning workers (before the implicit scope join), so work-stealing is effectively disabled: a worker that empties its own deque and gets one Empty/Retry steal pass exits instead of helping peers, defeating the documented load-balancing guarantee.
evidence: Confirmed against source: `done.store(true, Ordering::Release)` (line 1242) is the final statement of the `std::thread::scope` closure, on the spawning thread, immediately after the for-loop (lines 1191-1240) that spawns all `pl` workers. `std::thread::scope` runs the closure on the calling thread and only joins spawned threads when the closure RETURNS — so `done` becomes true essentially instantly, with no gating on actual work completion. A worker whose own deque empties (`worker.pop()` -> None, lines 1203-1205) makes a single steal pass over `my_stealers` (lines 1207-1212); if all return Empty/Retry it reaches the `done.load` check (line 1216), sees true, and breaks. Thus any worker that drains its round-robin-seeded items faster than a peer exits rather than stealing the peer's backlog, contradicting the doc comment 'thieves steal across them during imbalanced levels' (lines 1100-1101) and the O((m+n)/P + D·logP) bound (line 1103); it degrades to static round-robin partitioning. Correctness is preserved: `worker.pop()` is checked first each iteration and (per ws-deque pop_lifo) only returns None when the deque is truly empty — the last-element CAS race hands the item to a thief who processes it — and children are pushed to thread-local `local_out` (line 1232), never re-seeded onto deques, so no seeded item is dropped and the live set is correct. The identical premature-`done` pattern also exists in peony-emit/src/lib.rs (lines 300/329/365), corroborating the candidate's 'same pattern' framing.

summary: Input sections named `.got`/`.interp`/`.hash`/etc. are silently dropped: `place()` assigns them `SecSource::Got`/`Interp`/etc. based purely on output-section name, so emit never copies their input bytes.
evidence: Two independent code paths produce builders with the same reserved names. (1) The input-grouping loop (lines 341–398) buckets any allocatable input section named `.got` into `rw_pb` with real `contributions`. (2) The synthetic-section injection (lines 420–433 for `.got`, lines 484–506 for `.interp`/`.hash`/`.dynsym`/`.dynstr`/`.rela.dyn`) pushes *separate* builders with `contributions: Vec::new()` into the same vectors. `place()` then converts every builder whose `.name` matches a reserved string to the corresponding `SecSource` variant (lines 1298–1312), regardless of whether it came from inputs. In emit (peony-emit/src/lib.rs line 220–249), `SecSource::Got` writes GOT slot values, `SecSource::Interp` writes `layout.dyn_blobs.interp`, etc. — none of them iterate `sec.contributions`, so the input bytes from the first builder are silently discarded. `is_allocatable()` (lines 1499–1511) does NOT filter out `.got`, `.interp`, `.hash`, `.dynsym`, `.dynstr`, `.plt`, `.dynamic`, `.rela.*`, `.got.plt` — it only filters `.eh_frame*` and `.note*`.

summary: section_references_moved() hard-codes three section names, allowing non-.text/.data/.rodata sections with moved relocation targets to be incorrectly marked Green
evidence: The function at lines 435-442 returns true only when section_name is exactly ".text", ".data", or ".rodata" and moved is non-empty. For all other sections (e.g., ".got", ".init_array", ".data.rel.ro", ".tdata"), it returns false even if those sections contain relocations against moved symbols. In compute_red_green (line 422), a section is colored Green when its fingerprint matches AND section_references_moved returns false. If a section like .got has unchanged pre-relocation bytes but contains a relocation whose target symbol moved, the relocated bytes in .got will differ, but the section would be incorrectly colored Green ("skip re-emit", line 15). This violates the red-green contract stated at lines 369-371: sections with moved relocation targets must be Red.

summary: `try_reuse` never reads `manifest.epoch_key`, so the documented O(1) epoch fast path is absent; every reuse check re-hashes all input files. Compounding this, `record_link_with_sections` writes `epoch_key` with a hard-coded `args_hash = 0`, so the stored epoch key never reflects actual build arguments — making the field doubly dead.
evidence: Lines 283–286 document: "Fast path: compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file." The body of `try_reuse` (lines 287–324) decodes the manifest, checks `manifest.version` (line 301) and `manifest.inputs.len()` (line 306), then immediately iterates calling `Fingerprint::of_file(cur)` (line 313) for every input. `manifest.epoch_key` is never accessed. Separately, `record_link_with_sections` (line 352) persists `epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0)` with a hard-coded `args_hash = 0` rather than passing a real args hash, so even if a fast-path check were added, it would still be blind to argument changes.

summary: On relocation error, emit_full returns Err after having already written ELF headers and partial section data into the mmap, leaving a corrupt output file on disk with no cleanup.
evidence: Confirmed in source: `write_headers` (line 122) and `write_section_data_parallel` (line 127) both mutate the mmap before the `?` propagates a `RelocError`. The `mmap.flush()` at line 132 is bypassed on the error path. Inspection of memmap2 0.9.10 (`unix.rs:489-498`) confirms `Drop` calls only `munmap`, with no `msync`/flush — but `munmap` on Linux still marks pages for writeback by the page cache, so dirty bytes reach the file. The caller in `peony/src/main.rs:248-249` performs no `std::fs::remove_file` on error. For the `can_overwrite=false` path the file was freshly `create(true).truncate(true)` and sized with `set_len` (lines 102-109); for the in-place path (lines 93-99) the prior binary is partially overwritten. Either way, after a reloc error the file at `output_path` contains a non-functional ELF: a valid header pointing at sections whose relocations were only partially applied.

summary: peony-reloc has zero unit tests; patch_buf relocation arithmetic (20+ distinct formulas) is only validated end-to-end via integration tests skipped when system toolchain is absent
evidence: peony-reloc/src/lib.rs contains 0 #[test] functions (confirmed via grep). The patch_buf function (line 298-395) implements 19 distinct relocation type handlers covering: R64, PC64, GOTOFF64, SIZE64, R32/R32S, PC32, SIZE32, PLT32, GOTPCREL/GOTPCRELX/REX_GOTPCRELX, GOT32, GOTPC32, TPOFF32/TPOFF64, DTPOFF32/DTPOFF64, R16/PC16, R8/PC8. Each computes hand-written arithmetic using wrapping_add/sub operations with computed addresses (symbol VA, GOT base, TLS offset, etc.). Test coverage is limited to end-to-end integration tests in peony/tests/relocations.rs and peony/tests/tls.rs, which: (1) assemble x86-64 via the system `as` toolchain, (2) link with peony, (3) execute the binary and inspect exit codes. This means: tests are skipped if the system lacks `as`/cc/ld; patch_buf is never called directly with known inputs/outputs for verification; arithmetic errors (e.g., incorrect sign in GOTOFF64 = S+A-got_base, or negation order in TPOFF32 = tls+A-tls_size) would only be caught if they change the final exit code, not through isolated value checks.

summary: write_u32 and related overflow guards lack test coverage; RelocError::Overflow is never asserted in any test
evidence: Line 429 contains: `if (u as u32) as u64 != u && (val as i32) as i64 != val { return Err(overflow(...)) }`. This guard accepts values that fit as either zero-extended u32 OR sign-extended i32. grep confirms RelocError::Overflow is never constructed in tests (only defined at line 453 in peony-reloc/src/lib.rs). The test `mold_arch_x86_64_reloc_overflow` in peony/tests/mold_corpus.rs exists but is marked `#[ignore = "needs unsupported feature"]` and will not execute. No other test suite asserts the Overflow error path. Similar guard functions write_i32, write_u16, write_u8 (lines 403-449) have identical test coverage issues.

summary: Symbol kind (STT_FUNC / STT_OBJECT / STT_TLS) is never captured during parsing; every symbol in the output `.symtab` is emitted as `STT_NOTYPE`, breaking GDB function symbol detection, `nm` type fields, and profiler stack-frame identification.
evidence: In `parse_object` (lines 244-266), `InputSymbol` is constructed without calling `sym.kind()` (available from the `object 0.36` crate). The `InputSymbol` struct (lines 132-146) has no `sym_type` field, so the ELF `STT_FUNC`/`STT_OBJECT`/`STT_TLS` type bits are silently dropped at ingest time. Downstream, `build_symtab_plan` (peony-layout/src/lib.rs lines 1356, 1391, 312) hard-codes `elf::st_info(elf::STB_LOCAL, elf::STT_NOTYPE)` and `elf::st_info(bind, elf::STT_NOTYPE)` for every symbol — local and global alike — because no type information is available. `write_symtab` (peony-emit/src/lib.rs line 483) writes this `info` byte directly to the output symbol table. The `object` crate's `Symbol::kind()` method (which maps `ELF STT` values to `SymbolKind::Text → STT_FUNC`, `SymbolKind::Data → STT_OBJECT`, `SymbolKind::Tls → STT_TLS`, etc.) exists and is accessible but is never called. Concrete impact: (1) `readelf -s` and `nm` show every symbol as type `NOTYPE`; (2) GDB cannot automatically distinguish functions from data labels, breaking `info functions` and some breakpoint workflows; (3) profilers that use `STT_FUNC` to locate frame boundaries (e.g., Linux `perf`, Valgrind callgrind) will fail to identify function extents; (4) for dynamic links, copy relocations for `STT_OBJECT` imports require the size field to be correct — while size IS captured, the type mismatch can cause `ld.so` to mishandle copy relocs.

summary: build_id_hash hashes all input section bytes (including non-allocatable .debug_*, .symtab, relocation tables, etc.) over pre-relocation data, so the build-id does not identify the output binary; the same linked binary gets different build-ids when compiled with vs without debug info.
evidence: The build_id_hash function (lines 445-462) iterates unconditionally over all sections in objects → obj.sections without filtering. The object parsing (peony-object/src/lib.rs:216-238) includes ALL ELF sections (including .debug_*, relocation sections, .symtab, .strtab marked as SectionKind::Debug and SectionKind::Other). No SHF_ALLOC filtering is applied. The hash is computed in Phase 1 (line 239 of write_section_data_parallel), before Phase 2 applies relocations (lines 252-374). The hash uses raw pre-relocation s.data bytes. Concrete failures: (1) Compiling with -g vs -O2 produces identical loadable segments but different .debug_* bytes → different build-ids for the same runnable binary, defeating build-id's purpose for coredump-debuginfo matching. (2) Relocation sections (.rela.text, non-SHF_ALLOC) are included in the hash. (3) The hash is computed before apply_reloc (line 356) patches section data.

summary: `atomic_write` uses a fixed, process-independent temp path (`manifest.tmp`), creating a write-write race when two concurrent `peony` processes target the same output file with `--incremental` enabled.
evidence: At line 447, `let tmp = path.with_extension("tmp")` converts `<output>.incr/manifest.bin` → `<output>.incr/manifest.tmp`. This is a deterministic, process-independent path. If two `peony` invocations with `--incremental` run concurrently against the same `-o <output>` (e.g. a misconfigured parallel build rule), both call `std::fs::write(&tmp, data)` on the identical path. `fs::write` is not atomic (it truncates then writes in multiple syscalls), so interleaved writes produce a corrupt file. The subsequent `std::fs::rename(&tmp, path)` at line 452 is POSIX-atomic, so exactly one rename wins, but may commit a file containing bytes from both processes. No file locking or PID-based uniqueness guards this path; the crate has no `flock`/`Mutex`/`LockFile` dependency. Confirmed by: `manifest_path` (line 277-279) = `cache_dir(output).join("manifest.bin")`; `atomic_write` called only from `record_link_with_sections` (line 364); `record_link` called once per process at main.rs:252 after the full link completes.

summary: `write_u16` and `write_u8` hard-code the wrong `r_type` constant in their overflow error: `PC16` overflows are reported as `R16`, and `PC8` overflows are reported as `R8`, making relocation overflow errors misleading when diagnosing PC-relative reference failures.
evidence: `write_u16` (lines 436-442) constructs an overflow error via `overflow(object, reloc_off, val, r_x86_64::R16)`. It is called from two sites: the `R16` arm (line 385) and the `PC16` arm (line 386). The `PC16` call passes the same `r_x86_64::R16` constant (= 12) rather than `r_x86_64::PC16` (= 13), so a PC-relative 16-bit overflow reports the wrong type. Similarly, `write_u8` (lines 444-450) always uses `r_x86_64::R8` (= 14), but is also called from the `PC8` arm (line 388) where the correct constant would be `r_x86_64::PC8` (= 15). The `RelocError::Overflow` message on line 36 includes the `r_type` field (`'… for type {r_type}'`), so a user or tool seeing the error would look up the wrong relocation kind, leading them to diagnose the wrong relocation issue.

summary: Incremental cache key ignores linker arguments — changing args (e.g. --gc-sections, --entry) reuses a stale binary
evidence: Confirmed against source. try_reuse(output: &Path, inputs: &[PathBuf]) (lib.rs:287) accepts no argument representation and compares only manifest.version (301), per-input path+fingerprint pairs (309-317), and the output fingerprint (320-322). record_link_with_sections() computes epoch_key = compute_epoch_key(inputs, 0) (line 352) with args_hash HARDCODED to 0, and record_link() — the path the driver uses (main.rs:252) — funnels through it. The driver calls try_reuse(&args.output, &inputs) at main.rs:167 where `inputs` are only resolved positional file paths (main.rs:161-162, and parse_args sends only positional args to a.inputs at main.rs:132). Flags --gc-sections (main.rs:112) and --entry/-e (main.rs:106,130) change link output but never alter `inputs`. So `peony foo.o -o a.out --incremental` followed by `peony foo.o -o a.out --gc-sections --entry main --incremental` matches all checked fields (same input file, same fingerprint, unchanged output fingerprint) → try_reuse returns true → the previous binary, which does not reflect the new args, is silently reused. Additionally, try_reuse never even loads/compares manifest.epoch_key, so the advertised epoch-key fast path (doc lines 6-10) is doubly non-functional w.r.t. args. (Note: doc says SHA-256 but code uses FNV-1a; immaterial to this bug.)

summary: Relocation writers index buf[off..off+N] using an unvalidated reloc offset, panicking (slice out of bounds) on malformed/out-of-range relocation offsets in input object files.
evidence: Confirmed in source. write_u64 (peony-reloc/src/lib.rs:400 `buf[off..off+8]`), write_i32 (:415 `buf[off..off+4]`), write_u32 (:432), write_u16 (:440) and write_u8 (:448 `buf[off]`) only validate value width/overflow — never `off+N <= buf.len()`. `off` is `reloc.offset as usize`, set in apply_reloc (:292) and consumed in patch_buf (:300). reloc.offset is the raw ELF r_offset: peony-object/src/lib.rs:370-375 pushes `offset` straight from `object::section.relocations()` with no validation against the section's data size. In peony-emit, the slice passed to apply_reloc is exactly `data_len = isec.data.len()` bytes (lib.rs:341-350); the only guard there is `file_off + data_len > buf_len` (:342) for the section copy, not the reloc offset within the section. apply_reloc runs inside `std::thread::scope`/`scope.spawn` (peony-emit lib.rs:302/311), so a panic is re-raised on scope join and aborts the link after the output buffer is already created. By contrast the legacy serial writers guard explicitly: write_bytes (lib.rs:494 `if end <= buf.len()`), write_symtab (:468 `if off + 24 > buf.len() break`), GOT writer (:231 `if off + 8 <= buf.len()`). I reproduced the panic: `buf[12..20]` on a 16-byte buffer panics with slice-index out of bounds (catch_unwind confirms panicked=true).

summary: section_references_moved uses a hardcoded section-name heuristic that mis-colors output sections, producing both false-Green (unsound skip of re-emit) and false-Red (lost incremental reuse) results, contradicting the function's own documented red-green contract.
evidence: peony-cache/src/lib.rs:441 implements `section_references_moved` as `!moved.is_empty() && (section_name == ".text" || section_name == ".data" || section_name == ".rodata")`. compute_red_green (line 422) colors a section Green only when `prev_fp == *fp && !section_references_moved(name, &moved)`. (1) FALSE GREEN: any reloc-bearing output section not named exactly .text/.data/.rodata (e.g. .init_array, .fini_array, .got, .data.rel.ro, .tdata, .tbss) returns false, so an unchanged fingerprint yields Green = "skip re-emit" (doc line 91) even if it references a moved symbol — a stale/incorrect binary, directly violating the function doc (lines 370-371: Green requires "no relocation target moved"). (2) FALSE RED: when any symbol moved, every .text/.data/.rodata section returns true regardless of whether it actually references the moved symbol, forcing Red and defeating incremental reuse. The crate already defines RelocReverseIndex (lines 124-185) with iter_relocs(symbol_id) — the exact mechanism the comments (lines 432-440: "Heuristic", "conservative approximation", "A production implementation would use the relocation reverse index") say should be used but is not. Confirmed only test callers (peony/tests/algorithms.rs:280,311); no driver wiring, limiting present impact.

summary: Work-stealing loop collapses Steal::Retry into the no-op Empty arm, so a thief abandons a victim that demonstrably still has stealable work, degrading load balancing (not correctness).
evidence: peony-emit/src/lib.rs:321-324 matches `s.steal()` as `Steal::Success(v) => {..}` and `Steal::Empty | Steal::Retry => {}`. The same pattern appears in peony-layout/src/lib.rs:1207-1211. The ws-deque crate (this workspace's /home/cole/RustProjects/ws-deque/src/lib.rs:121-124) documents these as DISTINCT outcomes: `Empty` = "deque was observed empty"; `Retry` = "Lost a race with another thief or an emptying pop; the caller should retry" (i.e. the deque is non-empty). The crate even provides `is_retry()` (lib.rs:148-152) "so retry-loops port directly." By treating Retry as a no-op, the thief moves on (next stealer / spin_loop at line 330) instead of re-attempting the victim that still has work. Items are seeded round-robin (lines 296-298), so victims genuinely hold work, making lost-race Retry outcomes realistic. The candidate's claim that no work is lost is correct: each Worker drains its own deque via pop() (line 316) before stealing, and `done` is set (line 365) only after all threads are spawned, so every seeded item is still processed by its owner. The impact is purely reduced work-stealing effectiveness, not incorrectness.

summary: On-disk manifest is bincode-decoded with the limit-less `standard()` config, so a corrupt/hostile length field can trigger an unbounded eager allocation (abort/OOM) before validation.
evidence: try_reuse (lib.rs:296-300) and compute_red_green (392-401) both decode manifest.bin via `bincode::serde::decode_from_slice(&bytes, bincode::config::standard())`. Verified against bincode 2.0.1 (Cargo.lock:77-80) + serde 1.0.228: `standard()` resolves to `Configuration<..., NoLimit>` (no byte limit). The OOM path is the String fields inside `Manifest.inputs: Vec<(String, Fingerprint)>` — NOT the outer Vec. The outer Vec uses bincode's serde `deserialize_seq`, and serde's `Vec::deserialize` clamps pre-allocation via `size_hint::cautious` (MAX_PREALLOC_BYTES = 1 MiB), so a giant outer-Vec length only fails mid-read. But each `String` routes through bincode's `deserialize_string` -> native `Vec<u8>::decode`, which does `let mut vec = alloc::vec![0u8; len];` with `len = decode_slice_len()` read straight from the untrusted file. The protective `claim_container_read` is a no-op under NoLimit (de/mod.rs:182-191: `if LIMIT.is_some() {...} else { Ok(()) }`). So a manifest with a String length of ~2^60 forces an immediate `vec![0u8; 2^60]` -> allocation failure/abort, instead of the intended graceful fall-back to a full link. Two inaccuracies in the original candidate: (1) the 2^60 example was attributed to a `Vec` length, but the unbounded path is the inner `String`; the outer Vec is in fact cautious-clamped; (2) the claim that 'QUAD/SPEC notes call out untrusted-input deserialization' is unsupported — no such note exists in research/SPEC.md or the digests.

summary: Documented epoch-key fast path is never executed; try_reuse always re-fingerprints every input file
evidence: Module-level docs (lines 5–10) and the try_reuse doc comment (lines 283–286) promise an O(1) epoch-gated fast path: "compare the epoch key. If it matches, skip reading any input file." In practice, try_reuse (lines 287–324) never reads or compares manifest.epoch_key. After deserialising the manifest it goes straight to calling Fingerprint::of_file(cur) on every input (line 313) and Fingerprint::of_file(output) on the output (line 320), unconditionally reading and hashing every byte. The epoch_key field is written in record_link_with_sections (line 355) but the only code that reads it is in the test suite (lines 546–547). No production code path computes the current epoch key and compares it to the stored one before falling through to the full fingerprint loop.

summary: overwrite_in_place test relinks identical inputs, so it cannot catch stale-byte corruption from the non-zeroing overwrite-in-place fast path
evidence: Verified against the repo. `overwrite_in_place_produces_identical_output` (peony/tests/algorithms.rs:162-188) links the SAME object twice (obj.clone() at lines 177 and 182), so `bytes1 == bytes2` is trivially true regardless of correctness. In peony-emit/src/lib.rs the buffer is zeroed ONLY in the `!can_overwrite` branch (lines 117-119: `if !can_overwrite { mmap.iter_mut().for_each(|b| *b = 0); }`); the overwrite-in-place branch (can_overwrite==true when existing file size matches, lines 91-99) opens the existing file with O_RDWR and no truncate, reusing its prior contents WITHOUT zeroing. write_section_data_parallel (lines 270-352) writes only each section's `isec.data` range via copy_from_slice; alignment-gap/padding bytes — produced by the many `align_up(...)` calls in peony-layout/src/lib.rs (e.g. line 634 `align_up(file_content_end, 8)`) — plus BSS file-image regions are never rewritten and depend solely on zero-init. emit_full runs this path UNCONDITIONALLY (peony/src/main.rs:248), not just under --incremental; the cache only short-circuits when inputs are unchanged (main.rs:166). The other relink tests (incremental_partial_change in more_cases.rs:193, incremental_reuse_and_relink in link_and_run.rs:114) change a same-sized immediate operand so they DO hit the overwrite path, but they validate only via run() execution and never byte-diff against a fresh truncating link, so stale gap/padding bytes off the executed code path go undetected.

summary: No test exercises the relocation overflow / signed-unsigned range-check logic in write_i32/write_u32/write_u16/write_u8; the only corpus test for it is #[ignore]d and empty.
evidence: Verified in the repo: peony-reloc has zero #[test] functions and no tests module (grep -rc '#[test]' peony-reloc/ => ZERO). The width-checked writers carry subtle range logic that nothing exercises: write_i32 (lib.rs:411-413) checks `let v = val as i32; if v as i64 != val { return Err(overflow(...)) }`; write_u32 (lib.rs:419-434) accepts a value if EITHER the zero-extended u32 round-trips OR the sign-extended i32 round-trips: `if (u as u32) as u64 != u && (val as i32) as i64 != val { return Err(...) }`; write_u16 (436-442) and write_u8 (444-449) use the same dual i/u round-trip pattern. The overflow() helper and RelocError::Overflow variant exist (lib.rs:36, 452-453) but are never asserted by any test. The only x86_64 overflow corpus test, mold_arch_x86_64_reloc_overflow (peony/tests/mold_corpus.rs:492-495), is `#[ignore = \"needs unsupported feature\"]` with an empty body. Existing relocation integration tests in peony/tests/relocations.rs (abs64_and_pc32, pc64_cross_object, byte_reloc_r8 with small=7, combined_relocations) only drive the success path and assert exit codes; none pushes a value past the i32/u32/u16/u8 range to hit the error branches. A regression flipping && to || or dropping a branch in these comparisons would silently truncate an out-of-range PC32/PLT32/GOT/R32 value into the output with no test failing.

summary: Red-green coloring tests never pass a non-empty moved_symbols set, so the moved-symbol→Red path (the core Theorem 6.1 mechanism) is entirely untested.
evidence: Both red_green tests call compute_red_green(&fake_output, &current, &[]) with an empty moved_symbols slice: algorithms.rs:280 (red_green_all_green_when_nothing_changes, fn at line 246) and algorithms.rs:311 (red_green_changed_section_is_red, fn at line 288). A grep across the whole repo confirms these are the ONLY call sites of compute_red_green in tests, and section_references_moved (peony-cache/src/lib.rs:435) is called only from compute_red_green at lib.rs:422. With an empty slice, section_references_moved returns false immediately (lib.rs:436-438), so the guard `!section_references_moved(name, &moved)` at line 422 and the heuristic that forces .text/.data/.rodata to Red (lib.rs:441) are never exercised. There are no unit tests inside peony-cache/src/lib.rs covering this path either. A regression that disables moved-symbol invalidation (e.g. section_references_moved always returns false) would still pass both tests, yet would mark a fingerprint-identical section Green and skip re-emit even though a relocation target moved — the exact unsound case the cited Theorem 6.1 guards against.

summary: s3gc_chain_multiple_bfs_levels only asserts exit code 0 and never verifies dead sections are dropped, so an over-keeping GC regression is undetectable
evidence: Confirmed by reading the file. The test's docstring (lines 67-68) claims "All of B, C, D must be live; any extra sections beyond these four must be dead." The fixture (lines 73-109) defines a live chain fn_a→fn_b→fn_c→fn_d (rooted at _start) plus an unreferenced dead_fn in section .text.dead (lines 97-100). The ONLY assertion in the body is `assert_eq!(run(&out), 0, "chain GC binary must run and exit 0")` at line 113. The run() helper (common/mod.rs ~line 100) merely executes the binary and returns its exit code — it does no section/symbol inspection. _start (lines 104-108) calls fn_a then exits 0 via `xor %rdi,%rdi; syscall` REGARDLESS of GC behavior, and dead_fn is just a `ret`, so keeping it has no runtime effect. Consequently a GC bug that keeps ALL sections (or disables GC entirely) still exits 0 and passes. Unlike the sibling test s3gc_live_set_is_correct_and_smaller (lines 22-65) which links with and without --gc-sections and asserts `sz_gc < sz_no_gc`, this test does no size comparison and no readelf/symbol inspection, so its docstring claim that the live set is exactly correct is unverified.

summary: In write_section_data_parallel, every worker thread acquires the shared error_slot Mutex on every iteration of the inner work-stealing loop just to check is_some(), serializing all threads on a single futex even on the common no-error path; the read only needs to observe an error, so an AtomicBool sentinel (as already used for `done`) would make the hot path lock-free.
evidence: Confirmed by reading the source (no git HEAD exists; files are untracked, so the diff target is the current file contents). Line 314 reads exactly `if error_slot.lock().unwrap().is_some() { break; }` at the top of the `loop {` (line 312) inside fn write_section_data_parallel (line 203). error_slot is `Arc<std::sync::Mutex<Option<peony_reloc::RelocError>>>` (lines 289-290). The mutex is acquired and released unconditionally on every loop iteration, before the worker.pop()/steal at lines 316-334, i.e. once per section per thread on both error and no-error paths. std::sync::Mutex on Linux is a futex, so this is genuine cross-thread contention, not a trivial spinlock. The error WRITE path correctly locks at line 357 (`*error_slot.lock().unwrap() = Some(e)`), but the READ at line 314 needs no mutual exclusion. AtomicBool and Ordering are already imported (line 18) and the early-exit `done` flag already uses AtomicBool (line 300) with Ordering::Acquire/Release (lines 329, 365), proving the lock-free sentinel pattern is idiomatic here. Strong corroboration: the near-identical work-stealing loop in peony-layout/src/lib.rs (lines 1199-1239) accumulates into a per-thread local_out and locks `results` only once at the end (line 1238), with NO per-iteration mutex in its loop — confirming line 314 is an anomaly versus the codebase's own convention.

summary: WorkItem tuple's `data_len` field (index 1) is always hardcoded to `0usize` and silently discarded; the type comment falsely claims it holds the section data length, creating a latent maintainer trap
evidence: Line 266–267 defines the comment `// Work item: (file_offset, data_len, section_va, object_id, section_index)` and `type WorkItem = (usize, usize, u64, usize, usize)`. Line 275 constructs every work item as `(file_off, 0usize, section_va, c.object_id, c.section_index)` — the second field is always the literal `0`. Line 336 immediately discards it: `let (file_off, _, section_va, obj_id, sec_idx) = item;`. Line 341 then re-derives the actual length as `let data_len = isec.data.len();`. The stored `0` is never read. Meanwhile `SectionContribution.size: u64` (peony-layout/src/lib.rs:60) is available at the call site as `c.size`, making `c.size as usize` the natural value to store — but it is not used. The gap between the comment's promise and the always-zero value stored is invisible without reading the consumer, and unnamed tuple fields make it easy to overlook.

summary: PLT stub RIP-relative displacement is narrowed i64→i32 with an unchecked `as i32` cast and written directly into the stub, so a >2 GiB .plt/.got.plt separation would silently emit a wrong jmp target — unlike the round-trip-checked write_i32 in peony-reloc. Genuine robustness defect, but the candidate's stated trigger (large --base-address) is factually wrong.
evidence: Confirmed at peony-layout/src/lib.rs:847: `let disp = (slot_va as i64 - (stub_va as i64 + 6)) as i32;` followed by line 851 `stub[2..6].copy_from_slice(&disp.to_le_bytes())`, writing the truncated disp32 directly into the FF 25 (jmp *slot(%rip)) stub with no overflow check and no reloc-record path. By contrast peony-reloc/src/lib.rs:411-413 (write_i32) performs `let v = val as i32; if v as i64 != val { return Err(overflow(...)) }`. The arithmetic claim is correct: a displacement ≥2 GiB truncates to a wrong target. HOWEVER the candidate's feasibility reasoning is wrong: layout is strictly sequential from a single `base` (lines 593-617) — `.plt` is placed in the RX group (line 510) and `.got.plt` in the RW group (line 521), both growing from the same `base`. Thus `gotplt_base - plt_base` is bounded by intervening code/data size + page alignment, NOT by base_address (which shifts both equally and leaves their difference unchanged); the PIE path uses base 0. So the claimed trigger via large `--base-address` cannot occur; only a single output with >2 GiB of RX between .plt and .got.plt (a degenerate, never-realistically-produced binary) could overflow.

summary: parallel_emit_is_deterministic asserts only that 8 concurrent links produce byte-identical output; it never executes any binary, so a deterministic miscompile producing 8 identical-but-corrupt binaries passes silently.
evidence: Verified against source. peony/tests/algorithms.rs:121-155: the test spawns 8 threads, each calls link() then fs::read(&out), collects the byte vectors, and at lines 152-154 asserts only assert_eq!(first, r, ...) — pure byte-equality across the 8 outputs. There is no run() call anywhere in the test (grep of run( in algorithms.rs returns lines 58,113,187,376,382 — none inside 121-155). The fixture (lines 125-138) deterministically exits with code 99: `leaq x(%rip),%rax; movq $99,(%rax)` stores 99 into the .data word x, then `mov $60,%rax` (sys_exit), `movq x(%rip),%rdi` loads 99 into rdi, `syscall` → exit(99). The run() helper exists at peony/tests/common/mod.rs:100-106 and returns the process exit code as i32, so the candidate's proposed correctness check assert_eq!(run(&...), 99) is exactly right. The sibling test overwrite_in_place_produces_identical_output (lines 162-188) demonstrates the correct pattern: it byte-compares AND asserts assert_eq!(run(&out), 7) at line 187. Thus a deterministic relocation bug (double-applied RIP-relative PC32 patch, wrong section offset, or wrong ELF entry point) would yield 8 byte-identical corrupt binaries and pass all assertions undetected. Secondary: the comment at line 124 claims 'weak symbols' but the assembly has no .weak directives — only .globl x and .globl _start with two RIP-relative references — so the comment overstates the fixture's coverage.
